questions.q: Explain the difference between a kingdom and a phylum. How are they classified and why?
questions.answer: A kingdom is the highest level of classification in the six-kingdom system of classification. It includes all the organisms that share similar characteristics and are capable of reproducing among themselves. On the other hand, a phylum is a taxonomic rank that is below the kingdom level. It groups together organisms that have a common body structure and embryonic development. The main differences between the two are: (a) the level of classification (kingdom is the highest level, while phylum is below), (b) the characteristics used for classification (kingdoms are based on cell structure and reproduction, while phyla are based on body structure and embryonic development). The two are classified based on the following criteria: (a) the number of cells in the organism, (b) the presence or absence of a nucleus, (c) the method of reproduction, (d) the structure of the cell membrane, (e) the presence or absence of flagella, (f) the mode of nutrition, (g) the body structure, and (h) the embryonic development. The main reason for classifying organisms into kingdoms and phyla is to group similar organisms together and to understand their evolutionary relationships.
marking_scheme: Mention the difference between a kingdom and a phylum (1 mark)
Explain how kingdoms and phyla are classified (2 marks)
Justify the reasons for classifying organisms into kingdoms and phyla (2 marks)

questions.q: What are the main characteristics of prokaryotic cells? How are they different from eukaryotic cells?
questions.answer: The main characteristics of prokaryotic cells are: (a) the absence of a true nucleus, (b) the presence of a single circular chromosome, (c) the absence of membrane-bound organelles, (d) the presence of ribosomes, (e) the ability to reproduce by binary fission. Prokaryotic cells are different from eukaryotic cells in several ways: (a) cell structure (prokaryotic cells have a simple cell structure, while eukaryotic cells have a complex cell structure with membrane-bound organelles), (b) DNA organization (prokaryotic cells have a single circular chromosome, while eukaryotic cells have multiple linear chromosomes), (c) metabolic processes (prokaryotic cells have a simple metabolic process, while eukaryotic cells have a complex metabolic process with multiple organelles). The main reason for classifying cells into prokaryotic and eukaryotic is to understand the differences in their structure, function, and evolutionary relationships.
marking_scheme: List the main characteristics of prokaryotic cells (2 marks)
Explain how prokaryotic cells are different from eukaryotic cells (2 marks)
Justify the reasons for classifying cells into prokaryotic and eukaryotic (1 mark)

questions.q: Explain the concept of taxonomy. What are the different levels of classification in the six-kingdom system?
questions.answer: Taxonomy is the science of classifying living organisms into groups based on their characteristics and evolutionary relationships. The six-kingdom system of classification is based on the following levels of classification: (a) Kingdom: the highest level of classification, which includes all the organisms that share similar characteristics and are capable of reproducing among themselves, (b) Phylum: a taxonomic rank that is below the kingdom level, which groups together organisms that have a common body structure and embryonic development, (c) Class: a taxonomic rank that is below the phylum level, which groups together organisms that have similar characteristics and evolutionary relationships, (d) Order: a taxonomic rank that is below the class level, which groups together organisms that have similar characteristics and evolutionary relationships, (e) Family: a taxonomic rank that is below the order level, which groups together organisms that have similar characteristics and evolutionary relationships, (f) Genus: a taxonomic rank that is below the family level, which groups together organisms that have similar characteristics and evolutionary relationships, (g) Species: the lowest level of classification, which includes all the organisms that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. The main reason for classifying organisms into different levels is to understand their evolutionary relationships and to group similar organisms together.
marking_scheme: Define taxonomy and explain its importance (1 mark)
List the different levels of classification in the six-kingdom system (2 marks)
Justify the reasons for classifying organisms into different levels (2 marks)

questions.q: What are the main characteristics of monocots and dicots? How are they different from each other?
questions.answer: Monocots and dicots are two types of flowering plants that differ in their characteristics and features. Monocots have the following characteristics: (a) one cotyledon (seed leaf), (b) one seed leaf that is small and insignificant, (c) fibrous root system, (d) parallel veins in the leaves, (e) flowers with three or six parts. Dicots, on the other hand, have the following characteristics: (a) two cotyledons (seed leaves), (b) two seed leaves that are large and significant, (c) taproot system, (d) net-like veins in the leaves, (e) flowers with four or five parts. The main differences between monocots and dicots are: (a) the number of seed leaves (monocots have one, while dicots have two), (b) the root system (monocots have a fibrous root system, while dicots have a taproot system), (c) the venation of the leaves (monocots have parallel veins, while dicots have net-like veins). The main reason for classifying plants into monocots and dicots is to understand their evolutionary relationships and to group similar organisms together.
marking_scheme: List the main characteristics of monocots (2 marks)
List the main characteristics of dicots (2 marks)
Explain the differences between monocots and dicots (1 mark)

questions.q: Explain the concept of evolution by natural selection. What are the main mechanisms of evolution?
questions.answer: Evolution by natural selection is a process of evolution that occurs over many generations, resulting in the adaptation and survival of organisms with favorable traits. The main mechanisms of evolution are: (a) mutation: the process of genetic variation, (b) genetic drift: the change in the frequency of a gene in a population over time, (c) gene flow: the movement of genes from one population to another, (d) natural selection: the process of survival and reproduction of organisms with favorable traits. The main steps involved in evolution by natural selection are: (a) variation: the presence of genetic variation in a population, (b) mutation: the process of genetic variation, (c) genetic drift: the change in the frequency of a gene in a population over time, (d) gene flow: the movement of genes from one population to another, (e) natural selection: the process of survival and reproduction of organisms with favorable traits. The main reason for understanding evolution by natural selection is to understand the adaptations and survival of organisms in their environment.
marking_scheme: Define evolution by natural selection and explain its importance (1 mark)
List the main mechanisms of evolution (2 marks)
Explain the main steps involved in evolution by natural selection (2 marks)
